sections.title: Overview: Ways to Express the Future

content.subtitle: Same event, different forms
content.description: Often the same future event can be described in different ways. Which way you choose depends on function, how you see the future event, and level of formality.
examples: The shop closes at 3 pm tomorrow.
The shop will close at 3 pm tomorrow.
The shop is closing at 3 pm tomorrow.
The shop is going to close at 3 pm tomorrow.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Predictions
Future facts
Decisions made at the moment of speaking
Offers
Promises
Requests
Refusals (won't)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: It looks as if Jake will lose his job.
The factory will open in July.
I know! I'll ask for a pay rise tomorrow.
I'll help you with the advertising campaign.
I promise you you won't lose your job.
Will you give a presentation on the sales figures?
No, I won't give a presentation on the sales figures.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: For offers and suggestions with I and we, use shall: 'Shall I help you?' (offer), 'Shall we discuss this?' (suggestion).
For requests, would/could/can are more polite than will/can: 'Would/Could/Can you give a presentation?'
For refusals, couldn't/can't are possible: 'No, I couldn't give a presentation.'
In British English, shall is sometimes used with I/we instead of will: 'I shall be in touch again soon.'

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Predictions based on present evidence
Intentions

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Look at that wall. It looks as if it's going to fall down.
I'm going to get my degree, then get a well-paid job.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Arrangements
Intentions (but not usually for distant future)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I'm meeting Fiona on Friday to discuss the advertising campaign.
I'm asking for a pay rise tomorrow.
NOT usually: I'm working for a huge multinational one day. (Use 'going to work' instead)

content.subtitle: Use
items: Fixed future events (timetables, schedules)

content.subtitle: Example
items: The shop closes at 3 pm next Saturday.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Completed situations before a certain time
Continuing situations up to a certain time

content.subtitle: Examples
items: It looks as if Jake will have lost his job by the end of the week.
This time next month, I'll have worked at the company for exactly 25 years.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Continuing situations up to a certain time (emphasises duration)

content.subtitle: Example
items: This time next month, I'll have been working at the company for exactly 25 years.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Situations in progress at a certain time in the future
Situations which will happen in the normal course of events
Habits or repeated actions at a point in the future

content.subtitle: Examples
items: This time next week I'll be travelling round Russia on business.
The company Chairperson will be arriving on Thursday.
I think that, in the future, more and more people will be commuting to work by plane.

content.subtitle: Rule
content.description: After time words/phrases (when, while, once, as soon as, etc.), we do NOT use will or be going to. Instead, use present simple, present continuous, present perfect simple, or present perfect continuous.
examples: I'll give you a pay rise when you start working harder! (present simple)
I'll give you a pay rise once you're bringing in three new customers a week. (present continuous)
I'll give you a pay rise as soon as you've proved you're a hard worker. (present perfect simple)
I won't give you a pay rise until you've been working here for three years. (present perfect continuous)

sections.title: Other Ways to Express the Future

content.subtitle: be (just) about to
examples: I'm just about to ask for my pay rise.

content.subtitle: be (just) on the point/verge of
examples: I'm just on the point/verge of asking for my pay rise.

content.subtitle: be due to
examples: I'm due to meet my boss at eleven o'clock.

content.subtitle: be to do
examples: You're to get those reports written before Friday!
The factory is to open in July.

content.subtitle: other modals (might, etc.)
examples: I might ask for a pay rise tomorrow.

content.subtitle: Formation
content.description: When looking back at a past future, future verb forms become past: will → would, is going to → was going to, present simple → past simple, etc.
examples: Then: I think the factory will open in September. → Now: I thought the factory would open in September.
Then: I'm in a rush because the train leaves at 4. → Now: I was in a rush because the train left at 4.

sections.title: A: Circle the correct word or phrase (will vs. be going to vs. present continuous)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Sonia looks like she’s **falling / going to fall** asleep any minute!
When I leave school, I’m **being / going to be** a journalist.
When Jake’s got his degree, he’s **going / going to go** to drama school for a year.
Watch out or you’re **getting / going to get** into trouble!
When are they **realising / going to realise** that they have to improve their customer service?
When are you **opening / going to open** a branch in Littlehampton?
It sounds like we’re not having **/ going to have** a sales conference this year.
I think house prices are **falling / going to fall** in about six months.
Why is Tony **applying / going to apply** for that position? He’s not qualified at all.
We’re **discussing / going to discuss** this in detail in the next meeting.
I’m pretty sure that building’s **being / going to be** knocked down next week.
There’s no doubt the events of the last few days are **being / going to be** written about by future historians.
You’re not **handing / going to hand** in your resignation tomorrow, are you?
Fiona and Jerry aren’t **becoming / going to become** arrogant when they release their CD, are they?
The airline industry’s **having / going to have** to cut costs in the near future.

sections.title: B: Circle the correct word or phrase (will vs. present continuous vs. shall)
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: I can’t come to the beach this weekend, I’m afraid, as **we’ll visit / we’re visiting** my grandparents.
The Prime Minister **will answer / is answering** all of your questions in due course.
What time **does your plane arrive / is your plane arriving**?
items.answer: does your plane arrive / is your plane arriving

items.sentence: **I’ll look / I’m looking** after the neighbours’ kids tomorrow evening so I can’t come bowling.
**Shall / Will** we move on to the next item on the agenda now?
‘I don’t feel well at all.’ ‘**Are you going to / Will you be** sick?’
items.answer: Are you going to / Will you be

items.sentence: The last bus **leaves / is leaving** at midnight.
Karen, **shall / will** I give you a hand with that report?
I’m afraid **I’m not having / I won’t have** time to finish all this work today.
When **does she / is she going to** get round to having new business cards printed?

sections.title: C: Write a short phrase (will, be going to, present simple, present continuous)
sections.instructions: Write a short phrase using will, be going to, present simple or present continuous on the line. If more than one form is correct, write all possibilities.

items.sentence: **(I / come)** into the office on Saturday morning if you like, Pete.
Carol, **(you / show)** Harry how to use the photocopier, please?
items.answer: will you show / could you show

items.sentence: From the look of things, **(it / snow)** later today.
Hurry up! **(We / miss)** the ferry!
items.answer: We're going to miss / We'll miss

items.sentence: **(The winner / announce)** on 14th January.
items.answer: The winner is announced / will be announced / is going to be announced

items.sentence: I can’t come in to work this morning because **(I / go)** to the doctor’s.
Don’t worry! **(They / sack)** anyone for at least six months.
items.answer: They aren't going to sack / They won't sack

items.sentence: **(Jenny / find)** a job easily when she graduates?
items.answer: Will Jenny find / Is Jenny going to find

items.sentence: No, I’m afraid **(I / be)** at the meeting tomorrow.
**(The new version / launch)** in the spring.
items.answer: The new version launches / is launching / will launch / is going to launch

items.sentence: **(I / order)** a pizza, shall I?
It looks like **(some people / make)** redundant quite soon.
items.answer: some people are going to be made

items.sentence: What time **(the train / leave)** on Saturday?

sections.title: D: Future Perfect (correct or incorrect)
sections.instructions: If the phrase in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, write the correct form on the line so that the sentence is in the future perfect.

items.sentence: I think **we’ll have sold out** of these by the end of the day.
On Tuesday, Toby **will have been being** with the company for exactly 35 years.
**We’ll have been becoming** the market leader by the end of the summer.
At the end of the summer, **I’ll have taking** the same train to work every morning for 15 years.
When we meet tomorrow afternoon, **will you already have** a chance to look at the sales figures?
**You’ll have driven** for hours without a break when we get there, so you’ll need a rest before we go out.
A Managing Director **will have appointed** by the end of the month.
Sandra **won’t have been finished** the reports by then, but she should at least have started.
He definitely **will have been expecting** it.
**Will you have managed** to put something down in writing by the end of the day?
Why **will they have been waiting** for hours? I thought you’d told them we were going to leave later.
items.answer: will they have been waiting (✓ if context fits)

items.sentence: It sounds like the invitations **won’t all have delivering** by next Friday.

sections.title: E: Future Perfect Simple / Continuous / Future Continuous
sections.instructions: Write the verb in brackets in the correct form (future perfect simple, future perfect continuous or future continuous). If more than one form is correct, write all possibilities.

items.sentence: By the end of today ...... **(Ed / apply)** for over 17 jobs!
This time next year ...... **(I / hopefully / run)** my own business.
In three days’ time ...... **(we / live)** here for exactly two years.
items.answer: we will have been living / will have lived

items.sentence: ...... **(Gemma / already / arrive)** by the time we get there?
...... **(you / travel)** all day, will you? Surely ...... **(you / have)** several stops on the way, won’t you?
items.answer: Will you be travelling, you will have

items.sentence: ...... **(we / wait)** for you at reception when you arrive.
At the end of this sponsored jumpathon, ...... **(1,000 people / jump)** up and down on the spot non-stop for 48 hours!
...... **(the boys / sign)** autographs all morning, so they’ll need a couple of hours’ break before the recording session.
items.answer: The boys will have been signing

items.sentence: How many people ...... **(you / interview)** for the position tomorrow?
items.answer: will you be interviewing / will you interview

items.sentence: ...... **(Terry / see)** the e-mail as he’s only just got here so we’d better discuss that first.
What ...... **(you / do)** this time next week?
...... **(we / see)** Ken and Tricia when we go to the States as they’ll be away at the time, unfortunately.

sections.title: F: Time Clauses (similar meaning)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: The bell will ring and then you can all go home. You can all go home when ......
Ahmed will ask the questions and I’ll take notes. I’ll take notes while ......
You’re a trainee for three months and then you become a junior employee. You become a junior employee once ......
items.answer: you have been a trainee for three months

items.sentence: Miss Jones will be interviewing the candidates and Mr Dawkins will be recording the interviews. Miss Jones will be interviewing the candidates while ......
The sales figures will come in and then we’ll assess the situation again. We’ll assess the situation again as ......
items.answer: soon as the sales figures come in

items.sentence: Work for us for over a year and we’ll consider promoting you. We’ll only consider promoting you once ......
items.answer: you have worked for us for over a year

items.sentence: They’ll start selling in much larger quantities and then they’ll make a profit. They won’t make a profit until ......
items.answer: they start selling in much larger quantities

items.sentence: The publishing company will offer me a contract and then I’ll know for sure that they’re going to publish the book. I’ll know for sure that the book is going to be published when I ......
items.answer: have been offered a contract by the publishing company

sections.title: G: Information for job applicants (time clauses)
sections.instructions: Write a verb from the box in the correct form in each gap.

items.sentence: When you (1) ...... at the main gates, hand over the attached invitation letter.
You (2) ...... where to park, and how to get to reception.
As soon as you (3) ...... your name to the receptionist, you (4) ...... to Meeting Room 7.
While you (5) ...... in Meeting Room 7, please fill in one of the Personal Information forms.
When you (6) ...... for interview, please bring this form with you.
Your interview (7) ...... for approximately 45 minutes.
Please note that all interviews (8) ...... .
Once the selection process (9) ...... over, all recordings (10) ...... .
You (11) ...... as soon as a shortlist of candidates (12) ...... .
items.answer: will be notified, has been drawn up

sections.title: H: Sentence transformation (future expressions)
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. Use between three and eight words.

items.sentence: Shona will find out if she has been promoted yet. → Shona ...... (yet)
items.answer: has yet to find out if she has been promoted

items.sentence: They will decide very soon whether to close the Paris office. → They ...... (point)
items.answer: are on the point of deciding whether to close the Paris office

items.sentence: The plan is that I will give a presentation to the board on Friday morning. → I ...... (due)
items.answer: am due to give a presentation to the board on Friday morning

items.sentence: Are you going home in a minute? → Are you ...... (about)
Actually, there won’t be a general election for a while. → Actually, we ...... (verge)
items.answer: are not on the verge of a general election

items.sentence: What time do the inspectors arrive tomorrow? → What time ...... (due)
items.answer: are the inspectors due to arrive tomorrow

items.sentence: Keep this a secret. → You are ...... (to)
items.answer: not to tell anyone this secret / to keep this a secret

items.sentence: The government of France has decided to ban plastic bags. → The government of France ...... (banning)
items.answer: is to ban / is going to ban plastic bags

sections.title: I: Future in the past (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: The company ………………… going to expand, but the economic downturn has put a stop to that.
No one had any idea that Dave ………………… resign the next day.
We were all standing around in front of the TV, knowing that the Minister ………………… to make a statement on the emergency in the next few minutes.
Ann ………………… to find the documents soon, or her boss would find out she’d mislaid them.
………………… you going to apply for the post before you heard it had already been filled?
………………… Bill ………………… on a business trip the next day anyway, or did he have to pack a suitcase at the last minute?
You’re lucky you caught me. I ………………… just on the point of leaving.
We ………………… due to arrive at six, so I left a message saying we ………………… be there at nine.
I think they ………………… about to fire me when I quit!
Thompson understood the cryptic message. He was ………………… get to Berlin on the first flight.
We are pleased to announce that Keith Danish ...... replace Susan Williams as Operations Manager from 24th September. A does B is about to C is to D is due
I ...... Penny for her advice, but I'm not so sure that's such a good idea now. A am going to ask B was going to ask C was asking D will ask
Once ...... broadband, you won't want to go back to a dial-up connection. A you'll be using B you'll have used C you've been used D you've used
...... find out more about the costs before we make a final decision? A Do I B Will I C Shall I D Am I due to
...... to the Finance Manager or the Accounts Manager? A Will you be reporting B Will you have been reporting C Shall you be reporting D Are you on the verge of reporting
This time tomorrow, Barry will ...... across the Pacific for exactly three months. A be rowing B have rowed C be rowing D have been rowing
When ...... treating me with some respect at work? A will they have started B are they going to start C are they starting D will they be starting
Were you really just on the point ...... ? A of resigning B to resign C with resignation D of having resigned
